title: Chamomile
subtitle: Matricaria recutita, the kitchen's evening cup
excerpt: Single-herb profile. Botanical, traditional actions, key constituents, the western herbal canon's most-cited evening herb. The first materia-medica entry every reader should meet.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), chamomile entry, the western canonical reference, traditional uses across two centuries. Nicholas Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), the seventeenth-century English record of chamomile in domestic medicine. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), the Eclectic-American tradition's chamomile. EMA HMPC monograph on Matricaria flos (2015), modern conservative summary including dosing and safety. WHO Monograph on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1 (1999), cross-tradition evidence summary.

content.text: Chamomile is the western kitchen's end-of-day cup. It is recorded in almost every English-language home-medicine book of the last four centuries. The plant is a small annual with feathery leaves and white-petalled flowers with a yellow centre. The flowers carry the action. The kitchen meets them as dried whole flowers in the tea cup.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Chamomile is in the daisy family. If you react to ragweed or marigold, patch-test on the inner forearm before drinking. Wait 24 to 48 hours.

A cup of tea is fine for almost everyone. Strong daily tinctures may add to the effect of sedatives or benzodiazepines. Talk to your prescriber first if you take any.

A cup of tea in pregnancy is fine. Talk to a herbalist or midwife before strong daily tinctures.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

content.text: Botanical
German chamomile is the annual that the western herbal tradition takes as the default. Family
Asteraceae

, height 20 to 50 cm. Finely branched feathery leaves. Daisy-style flowers 1 to 2 cm wide in mid to late summer. The yellow centre is hollow when you split a flower open. That is the test that tells true chamomile from its lookalike scentless mayweed, which has a solid centre and almost no scent. True chamomile smells of apple and honey when the flowers are crushed. The smell is the second test.

Roman chamomile is the perennial sibling. A different species, with a more bitter cup and a stronger ground-cover habit. Western herbal practice uses both. This profile follows German chamomile, the annual that most modern open-access research uses as the reference plant.

Part used: the flowers, whole heads, dried in the dark to preserve the volatile oils. Harvesting in the garden takes the flowers in full open at midday on a dry day; commercial dried chamomile from a herbalist supplier is the kitchen norm.
Constituents and actions

The main constituents are the volatile oil, the flavonoids apigenin and luteolin, and a small set of coumarins. The volatile oil holds chamazulene, bisabolol, and the bisabolol oxides. Chamazulene is what turns chamomile essential oil deep blue. It forms during steam distillation rather than sitting in the dried flower as a blue compound.
Western herbal practice names four primary actions:
Chamomile is traditionally described as a
nervine

, taken in the evening to ease a wound-up day. The constituent apigenin is the one most often associated with this action in the modern open-access literature. Phrasing matters here: chamomile is not a sleeping pill and the publication does not present it as one.
It is also a
carminative

, the kitchen's after-meal cup for indigestion, mild bloating, and trapped wind. Old herbals credit the volatile oil with the settling action. The same action makes it the classic continental European baby remedy for the gentle settle after a feed (always at low strength, and always cleared with the GP first for infants under two).

Chamomile also calms swelling. Bisabolol and the flavonoids are the constituents behind this. They make chamomile a traditional outer compress for mild skin irritation. Cool compresses over closed eyelids ease sore eyes. A cooled-infusion mouth rinse soothes inflamed gums. Never use the rinse for a teething infant, as the rinse can be breathed in.

And a mild sedative, the action that makes a strong tincture (1-5 ml three times a day) a herbalist's choice for short-term sleep support. The publication's voice doesn't claim sedation from a culinary cup; the strong-tincture dose is the one the tradition uses for that action.
Traditional uses

Western herbal: old herbals record chamomile as a working-stable herb as well as a kitchen one. The cup after the evening meal, the cooled compress over the sore eye, the warm bath for a restless child with growing pains. Older books also describe it as a herb "for the breast", meaning the lungs. Drunk hot in the evening through cold-season weeks.

Folk European: chamomile is the German Heilpflanze des Jahres (medicinal plant of the year) of 1987, a recognition by the German herbal community of its place in continental domestic medicine. The Manzanilla of Spain, "little apple", names the herb for its apple-like smell. The same name appears across Iberia and Latin America for the same use.

Eclectic-American: the late 19th-century American dispensatory records the same main uses, with one addition. Chamomile as a cooled-infusion eye-wash for mild conjunctivitis. Apply it with a clean pad of cotton wool dipped in cooled tea. The eye use is a kitchen tradition with one careful caveat. See your GP for any eye problem that does not clear in 24 hours, and never apply a herbal preparation to an injured eye.
Preparations

Hot infusion is the kitchen default, 1-2 teaspoons of dried flowers in a covered cup, just-off-boil water, steeped 8-10 minutes. One cup in the evening, or after a meal that needs settling. The lid traps the volatile oils that carry the action; without the lid, the cup is half a remedy.

Strong infusion (1:10, double the standard ratio) is the base for an external compress, cooled to skin-tolerable, applied with a clean cloth over the area for 10-20 minutes. The same strong infusion, further diluted with water, is the mouth-rinse for inflamed gums.

Tincture (1:5 dried-herb-to-40%-spirit by weight, macerated 4-6 weeks) is the herbalist's preparation for the stronger sedative action and the most reliable long-term storage form. Dose 1-5 ml three times a day at therapeutic strength. Tinctures are not the publication's first recommendation for a beginner, the cup is. The tincture is the next step for readers who want a more concentrated form, and the entry-level case where the publication would suggest seeing a herbalist before increasing the strength on your own.

Bath: 50 g of dried flowers steeped 30 minutes in 1 litre of just-boiled water, strained, added to a warm bath. The traditional preparation for restless evenings and growing-pains aches in older children. Not for infants in any form.
Cautions and contraindications

Allergy: Asteraceae cross-reactivity. Readers with a known ragweed, daisy, chrysanthemum, or calendula allergy should patch-test before any new chamomile preparation. Test: dab a small amount on the inside of the forearm; wait 24-48 hours; look for redness, itch, or swelling. Reactions to chamomile are not common but they happen, and they're easier to head off in a patch-test than after a steaming cup.

Pregnancy and breastfeeding: a standard cup of tea (1 to 2 teaspoons per cup) is widely tolerated. Strong daily tinctures or double-strength tea are best discussed with a herbalist or your midwife rather than self-prescribed. The everyday cup has no record of harm. There is also no clear evidence that the stronger dose helps more than the cup. So in pregnancy, the cup is the dose to use.

Children: a standard cup of tea (1 teaspoon per cup, steeped 5 minutes) is widely tolerated from school age. One cup in the evening. For babies and toddlers, see a paediatric herbalist or your GP. Adult dose halved is not a safe child dose. The continental European tradition uses very weak warm chamomile to settle a baby after feeds, but only under a herbalist's care. Not a home recommendation.

Drug interactions: no notable interactions at tea-cup doses. A strong daily tincture may add to the effect of sedatives, benzodiazepines, or other drugs that slow the central nervous system. Talk to your prescriber before starting a daily tincture if you take any of these. Lab work suggests chamomile may thin the blood slightly. The clinical picture is unclear, but mention chamomile-tincture use to your prescriber if you take warfarin or any anti-clotting drug.

When to skip and see a doctor: any allergic reaction at the patch-test or after the first cup. Swelling around the mouth, hives, or any breathing change. Call 111 or 999 for any breathing change. See your GP rather than reach for the cup for: any sleep problem that lasts more than a month, sleep problems with low mood, snoring with daytime sleepiness, or any sudden change in sleep pattern in an older adult.
